Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural {{Japanese Hot Spring Resort |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" {{Japanese Hot Spring Resort |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori {{Japanese Hot Spring Resort |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{Japanese Hot Spring" to "{{infobox Onsen"
revision.text:
{{infobox Onsen Resort |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Prefecture]] * [[Misaso Onsen]] – Another radioactive hot spring nearby == External Links == * [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] * [https://www.sekigane-yurari.com Tottori Sekigane Onsen Yurarī] {{Tottori Prefecture Hot Springs}} {{Hot Spring}} {{citation needed|せきかねおんせん}} [[Category:Hot Springs in Tottori Prefecture]] An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go {{infobox Onsen |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Prefecture]] * [[Misaso Onsen]] – Another radioactive hot spring nearby == External Links == * [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] * {{infobox Onsen |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Prefecture]] * [[Misaso Onsen]] – Another radioactive hot spring nearby == External Links == * [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] * (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Prefecture]] * [[Misaso Onsen]] – Another radioactive hot spring nearby == External Links == * [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] * [https://www.sekigane-yurari.com Tottori Sekigane Onsen Yurarī] {{Tottori Prefecture Hot Springs}} {{citation needed|せきかねおんせん}} {{Onsen}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hot Springs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:National Recreational Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Geography of Kurayoshi City]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Radioactive spring]] [[Category:Radioactive spring forks]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Sekigane Onsen]] [[ja:関金温泉]] [[ja:有馬温泉]] [[ja:増富温泉]] [[ja:玉川温泉 (秋田県)]] [[ja:母畑温泉]] [[ja:猫啼温泉]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sekigane Onsen}}

revision.text:
{{infobox Onsen |Name = Sekigane Onsen |image = File:Sekigane onsen Yumeikan.jpg |Comment = Sekigane Onsen Yumemyōkan |Latitude Degrees = 35 |Latitude Minutes = 21 |Latitude Seconds = 44.97 |Longitude Degrees = 133 |Longitude Minutes = 45 |Longitude Seconds = 46.21 |Location = {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市|12=draft}} Sekigane-machi, Sekigane-yado |Access = : By car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道|12=draft}} {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原IC|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号|12=draft}} toward Kurayoshi
: By rail and local bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West|12=draft}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅|12=draft}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス|12=draft}} for about 35 minutes |Water Type = {{ill|Radiation Hot Spring|ja|放射能泉|12=draft}} |Temperature = 40 - 62 |Discharge = 337 L/min |pH = |Liquid Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Accommodations = 5 |Total Capacity = |Annual Bath Visitors = 23,410 |Statistical Year = 2017 |External links = [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] |Special Notes = }} {{Location map | Japan Tottori | lat_deg = 35 | lat_min = 21 | lat_sec = 44.97 | lat_dir = N | lon_deg = 133 | lon_min = 45 | lon_sec = 46.21 | lon_dir = E | label = Location of Sekigane Onsen | position = bottom | mark = Map pointer.svg | marksize = 9 | width = 300 | float = right | caption = Location of Sekigane Onsen | alt = Location of Sekigane Onsen }} '''Sekigane Onsen''' (せきがねおんせん) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|ja|鳥取県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kurayoshi City|ja|倉吉市|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hōki Province|ja|伯耆国|12=draft}}). == Water Quality == It is a {{ill|radiation hot spring|ja|放射能泉|12=draft}}, ranking second in Japan after {{ill|Misaso Onsen|ja|三朝温泉|12=draft}} in its content of {{ill|radon|ja|ラドン|12=draft}} and other radioactive elements. Its water is colorless and odorless, renowned as a "beautiful hot spring." Although the reason why both Sekigane Onsen and Misaso Onsen exhibit radioactivity was long unknown, it was determined in 1955 (Showa 30) that a water-formed {{ill|uranium|ja|ウラン|12=draft}} mineral deposit discovered nearby is the source. The source temperature is between 40 and 62℃. == Onsen Town == {{File:Sekigane onsen YURARI.jpg|thumb|Sekigane Onsen Yurarī}} The hot spring town extends along the eastern foothills of {{ill|Daisen|ja|大山|12=draft}}, located deep from {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号|12=draft}}. There are five ryokan in the area. One public bath, "Kan no Yu," is available, and as a day-use facility, "Sekigane Yumemyōkan" is also in operation. The town comprises both an old onsen district and a new district developed in 1963 (Showa 38) that draws water from a municipal source. When the area was designated as a {{ill|National Recreational Hot Spring Resort|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=draft}} on March 24, 1969 (Showa 45), local regulations were enacted to centralize management of the hot spring sources. An old national lodging facility, Green School Sekigane, once existed here. It has been renovated and reopened as {{ill|HOTEL Hoshitori Terrace Sekigane|ja|HOTEL星取テラスせきがね|12=draft}} on April 26, 2025 (Reiwa 7)[https://www.sanin-chuo.co.jp/articles/-/717807 Kurayoshi's old national lodging renewed; Sekigane hotel to open on April 26 with universal design adoption] (Shanin Chūō Shinpo) January 24, 2025.. == History == Various legends exist regarding the founding of the hot spring. Some sources attribute its opening to the Yōrō era (717–723 AD), others to the Enryaku era (782–806 AD) as noted in the Japanese Hot Spring Chronicles, while still others attribute it to the 12th year of the Jōgan era (870 AD) or even to the Genkō era (1331–1333 AD). One legend recounts that when a crane was seen bathing, the monk 【行基】 discovered the spring, and 【Kōbō Daishi (Kūkai)】 later developed the desolate hot spring area. Some accounts even credit the discovery to Kūkai. According to the Genkō-era version, the lord of Sekigane Castle, Yamana Kotarō, is said to have opened the springYamana Kotarō was a member of the Yamana clan, guardians of Hōki, who was said to have been killed in battle at Sekigane during a succession dispute. However, this incident likely occurred in the late 15th century, which is significantly later than the Genkō era (mid-14th century).. Additionally, early Showa-era literature states that Gyōki (668–749) discovered the spring, and after a period of decline, Kūkai (774–835) revived it, with Yamana Kotarō (14??–1489?) installing the bath.. Sekigane Onsen is also known by the ancient names "Ginnoyu" and "Shiroganeyu" due to the beauty of its waters, and it appears as "Ginyu" in the Hōki Mindanki. During the Edo period, it was located along the 【Binchū Kaidō】 (also known as the Mimasaka Kaidō) connecting central Tottori with the Sanyō region, and a post station, Sekigane-juku, was established there. Prior to the era when radiation was measured, the water was described as a pale yellow, odorless carbonated spring. According to surveys conducted by the Tottori Prefecture based on the admission tax, the number of users peaked at around 34,000 in 2003 (Heisei 15). Usage then declined to approximately 13,000 in 2012, but has since rebounded to around 23,000 in 2017. Estimates based on municipal reports prior to 1997 (Heisei 9) suggest that during the bubble economy, annual usage might have been around 400,000 visitors. == Events and Festivals == In this region, traditional customs such as the imperial procession on October 7 and rice-planting songs (denoted in the "Daisenji Engi Emaki") are observed. * The "Sekigane Satomi Festival" is held on the first Sunday of September. == Nearby Attractions == * '''[[Daikatsuyama Jizōin (Kurayoshi)|Daikatsuyama Jizōin]]''' – Historically known as "Sekino Jizō" in Sekigane Onsen. The wooden half-seated Jizō Bosatsu statue here is designated as a nationally important cultural property, indicating that the Jizōin was established in 1192 (Kenkyu 3) by Sasaki Takatsuna. (Note that the current structure is a reconstruction.) Other cultural assets, such as a Giboju and a Song dynasty celadon incense burner, are also preserved. * {{ill|Yukase Shrine|ja|湯関神社|12=draft}} * The ruins of {{ill|Kamei Castle|ja|亀井城跡|12=draft}} == Access == * By Car: Approximately 35 minutes from {{ill|Yonago Expressway|ja|米子自動車道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yubaru Interchange|ja|湯原インターチェンジ|12=draft}} via {{ill|National Route 313|ja|国道313号|12=draft}} toward Kurayoshi. * By Rail and Bus: From {{ill|JR West|ja|西日本旅客鉄道|lt=JR West|12=draft}} {{ill|Sanin Main Line|ja|山陰本線|12=draft}} {{ill|Kurayoshi Station|ja|倉吉駅|12=draft}}, take a {{ill|Nikkō Kōtsu Bus|ja|日交バス|12=draft}} for about 35 minutes. : Previously, a branch of the {{ill|Kurayoshi Line|ja|倉吉線|12=draft}} served Sekigane Station as the nearest stop, but the line was abolished on April 1, 1985 (Showa 60) and the station was removed. : Although bus schedules and frequencies are limited, it is also possible to access the area by taking a community bus ("Maniwakun") on the 37 "Harsan-Kūse Route" from the {{ill|Himeshin Line|ja|姫新線|12=draft}}/{{ill|Chūgoku Katsuya Station|ja|中国勝山駅|12=draft}}, transferring at Miyata Stop onto the 32 "Chūso-Sekigane Route." * By Highway Bus: Services such as the {{ill|Shin-Kurayoshi Kaidō Express|ja|新倉吉街道エクスプレス|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Meribird-go (Hiroshima - Tottori Line)|ja|メリーバード号 (広島 - 鳥取線)|12=draft}} operate. == Footnotes and References == {{Reflist |refs= *[http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226510/h22toukei.pdf Tottori Prefecture Cultural Tourism Bureau, Tourism Policy Division: Visitor Dynamics Survey (Heisei 22)] (accessed September 9, 2014). * [http://www.pref.tottori.lg.jp/secure/226511/3003onsenirikomi.pdf Tottori Prefecture Tourism Exchange Bureau, Tourism Strategy Division: Hot Spring Visitor Numbers (March, Heisei 30)] (accessed June 7, 2018). *『全国温泉大事典』, pp.560–563 *『鳥取県の地名（日本歴史地名大系）』, pp.492–500 "Sekigane-machi" *『日本地名大辞典 31 鳥取県（角川日本地名大辞典）』, p.436 "Sekigane Onsen" *『全國温泉案内』, pp.443–453 *『溫泉案内』, pp.216–227 *『図説日本文化地理大系4 中国1』, pp.334–335 *『日本の文化地理　第13巻 兵庫・岡山・鳥取』, pp.251–252 *『鳥取県の歴史散歩』, pp.158–160 *[http://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp 国指定文化財等データベース: Wooden Jizō Bosatsu Half-seated Statue] (accessed September 25, 2014). }} == See Also == * [[Tourist Attractions in Tottori Prefecture]] * [[Misaso Onsen]] – Another radioactive hot spring nearby == External Links == * [http://www.apionet.or.jp/kankou/ Kurayoshi Tourism Mice Association – Kurayoshi Tourism Information] * [https://www.sekigane-yurari.com Tottori Sekigane Onsen Yurarī] {{Tottori Prefecture Hot Springs}} {{citation needed|せきかねおんせん}} {{Onsen}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Hot Springs in Tottori Prefecture]] [[Category:National Recreational Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Geography of Kurayoshi City]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:completion tier 2]] [[Category:Radioactive spring]] [[Category:Radioactive spring forks]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Sekigane Onsen]] [[ja:関金温泉]] [[ja:有馬温泉]] [[ja:増富温泉]] [[ja:玉川温泉 (秋田県)]] [[ja:母畑温泉]] [[ja:猫啼温泉]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sekigane Onsen}}
